posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Ponder this the next time you’re worrying about a case of wine sitting in an 85-degree warehouse for a week.

Decanted and served double-blind to my brown-bag group.

Mature scents but fresh. Old enough that it’s hard to divine the grape. In the mouth, there’s some oxidation, but not enough to spoil it; the wine is still quite sound. Somewhat attenuated fruit, but there’s something sweet and caressing there, even though it’s a bit faded. The finish is a bit thin, with a tad too much acid showing.

By no means a great wine, but it still gives some pleasure. A decade or more past its prime, I thought, but quite alive.

When it was unveiled it turned out to be a 1961 Ch. Beausejour St. Emilion – before the property was split in two as Duffau Lagarrosse and Becot.

What was remarkable was that wine had been stored in our host’s closet in his NYC apartment since the mid-60s – no temperature control whatsoever.

I’m sure this wine would have been outstanding if it had been properly stored. And I suspect it was superb after 25 or 30 years in the closet, even if 55 years was pushing it. Its warmth (of flavor, not storage temperature) spoke of the vintage, I think (it’s been ages since I had a '61 Bordeaux).

I hereby resolve to be less neurotic about storage.

posts.name: Richard T r i m p i
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

…What was remarkable was that wine had been stored in our host’s closet in his NYC apartment since the mid-60s – no temperature control whatsoever. I’m sure this wine would have been outstanding if it had been properly stored. And I suspect it was superb after 25 or 30 years in the closet, even if 55 years was pushing it… I hereby resolve to be less neurotic about storage.

No, no, no. 56F +/- 2 degrees…MANDATORY. 2 minutes at 75F, you might as well boil it.

Obviously it was bullet-proof Bordeaux…if it were Burgundy, it would’ve been destroyed that first Summer.

You got crazy lucky.

RT
posts.display_username: Richard T r i m p i

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
John,

I am all in for a repeat of my “aging experiment” where I took identical wines and stored them in the wine cellar (53), the basement (54-72 variance over the course of the year) and an upstairs closet (58-80 over the course of the year) for 2 years then did a blind tasting. While the upstairs wines were more advanced they were by no means ruined or even compromised, just further along the curve. Nobody could tell the difference between the cellar and basement wines. I did that with 2006 Gysler Riesling Kabinett, 2005 Pepiere Muscadet Briords and 2005 Faiveley Mercurey Framboisiere. Dale Williams did a similar thing with some other wines, and had similar results.

I would love to get a group together that would commit to a 3-5 year experiment.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: I have always believed that structured red wines are far more durable than most of us think. Living in Florida, and shipping wine from out of state, you invariably expose your wines to temperatures above 70°. While I try to set 80° as my upper limit, I’m sure some of the wine I’ve acquired over the years have been exposed to warmer temperatures. I have not seen any particular pattern where my wines show temperature exposure concerns. Still tough not to stress about it even just a little bit. We invest so much money, time and effort into this great passion.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
D@vid Bu3ker:

I would love to get a group together that would commit to a 3-5 year experiment.

Last night was sort of a 55-year experiment.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Yeah - I am not that patient.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
And it would require some implausible actuarial assumptions, at least in my case.

To be fair, long term storage conditions do matter. Some years ago in this same group – in the early 2000s, I believe – the guy who hosted last night and another member both served 1990 JJ Prum Wehlener Ausleses. They’d split a case when it was released. Per our format, neither knew what the other was serving.

The closet-stored bottle was fine, but the other bottle, which had been in temperature-controlled storage, markedly better.

(Of course, storage isn’t the only variable. Two or three years ago, I and another member each served the same Willi Shaeffer Graacher Kabinett back to back. Each bottle was purchased at Crush a day or two before the tasting. No one thought they were the same wine when served. They were very different. No storage issue there. Go figure!)

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked: Do you know what the fill was?

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

When it was unveiled it turned out to be a 1961 Ch. Beausejour St. Emilion – before the property was split in two as Duffau Lagarrosse and Becot.

Actually, I think the property was divided in the late 1800’s. Learn about Chateau Beausejour Complete Guide

FWIW, I have a friend of mine that lives at the beach, so they have constant humidity and cooler temperatures. They purchased wine in the 50’s and 60’s, stored them under their bed, and decades later, the wines have turned out well. But I imagine, the wines would have been even better if well stored.

link_counts.title: Learn about Chateau Beausejour Duffau-Lagarrosse Complete Guide

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
My bad on the split date. I did in fact consult your site, Jeff, but I misread 1869 as 1969 because I was thinking the split was in the 20th century. The fact that the label didn’t have either family name led me to think the split was post-1961.

This was the Becot property, Beau-Sejour, even though that’s not on this label and there is no hyphen in the name. Owner Jean Fagouet’s name is in the bottom right.

to be the truth…as told to us by all the wine experts. These crackpot ideas about how
wine can survive such brutal treatment are absolute heresy. If everyone believed such
hare-brained idea…it would eliminate all these threads ripping FedEx and UPS for delivering
wine when the temps are in the 80’s. And then where would all these folks go to get on their
high-horse. You guys are evil.
Tom

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: It could be also that wines from era were made in a more ‘fault tolerant’ way – press juice, pips, stems, higher acid etc. So they could survive the war crime worthy treatment of an uncooled closet, but perhaps todays coddled optically sorted, Montessori raised, Yanni elevaged wines can’t?

posts.name: Faryan Amir-Ghassemi
posts.cooked:
John Morris:
D@vid Bu3ker:

I would love to get a group together that would commit to a 3-5 year experiment.

Last night was sort of a 55-year experiment.

To be clear, an uncontrolled experiment of marginal p value

Agree with the broad sentiment though.

Great stuff

When I took over a large restaurant (ex-Grand Award Winner) in 2008, there were a few wines that were inexplicably stored in racks near heat vents that would blow throughout the Winter. One of the first things I did was block off those racks from containing anything but show bottles (the wine storage was part of the decor in private rooms) and pull certain bottles that showed clear signs of seepage or lower fill levels.

It was interesting to see how many of them at age 10 and stored so poorly actually showed well. Some Arcadian 98 Pinot was outstanding, and they were all low fills and soaked corks. I took an early 90s Beringer PR to a dinner to compare with a bottle bought on release and stored properly, and there was a difference, but the poorly-stored one was perfectly enjoyable if a little less focused and a little more mature. Who knows? Maybe it was the cork and not the storage (it was probably the storage).

I did pour out the Kenwood Artist Series vertical from the 80s, though. Every bottle was pretty much shot and leaky. They were closest to a vent, and that thing had to blow a constant heat to keep those big rooms warm.

Anyway, obviously anecdotal, but it did make me think wine was tougher than we think. I’ve always thought because our own storage was one of the few variables we have control over in wine that we tend to obsess over it more.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Circa 1994, someone served me a 1975 cru bourgeois Bordeaux that he had kept in his NYC townhouse since release. I was surprised how sound it was. Of course, 75 was a pretty tannic year, so the natural structure might have helped.

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
So, because one old bottle of Bordeaux was kind of OK after years of sub-optimal storage, we should all store our best wines in a crappy closet?

While I think it’s possible to fret needlessly about “perfect” storage conditions, that doesn’t mean that proper storage is irrelevant. Over the years, I’ve tasted wines stored under a wide variety of conditions. Some of the bottles I’ve tasted that “should” have been decent were completely shot when stored in cruddy conditions.

Bruce

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: Was hanging at my wife’s friends place one night…she had this faux fire place with a bunch of wine bottles in front…I didn’t think much of it initially…later in the night I went over and started inspecting some of the bottles because I realized they were full…my wifes friend begins to say they had been sitting in a garage engulfed by hot Florida heat for quite sometime and never stored correctly…oh well, all of a sudden I see hidden deep within the lonely gathering of bottles an '83 ducru beaucaillou…immediately I exclaim, “holy fuq, do u what u have here?” She has no clue…I pretty much tell we r opening it and she has no choice…lol…we had zero expectations…I tell u what, this wine must of been incredible at one time because though it was well past it’s prime, it was still drinkable, you could feel the structure still…it felt like a 40-50 year wine if stored properly. A cool experience regardless.

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

So, because one old bottle of Bordeaux was kind of OK after years of sub-optimal storage, we should all store our best wines in a crappy closet?

No one is saying that, Bruce.

Well, then what are you saying? Here’s your tasting note:

"Mature scents but fresh. Old enough that it’s hard to divine the grape. In the mouth, there’s some oxidation, but not enough to spoil it; the wine is still quite sound. Somewhat attenuated fruit, but there’s something sweet and caressing there, even though it’s a bit faded. The finish is a bit thin, with a tad too much acid showing. "

Just taking this single data point, that’s hardly a ringing endorsement of sub-optimal storage conditions, especially over decades. It sounds like a wine that has barely survived, and very possibly could have been much better if stored properly.

Bruce

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
An interesting question to me is whether there is a point along the aging curve where suboptimal storage conditions make a wine better. The answer to this question will differ for each palate, but imagine a graph where age is the horizontal axis and personally perceived quality (say, points you would give) is the vertical axis. Now chart out the age-quality curve for two bottles of an identical wine, one poorly stored and one well stored. I have little doubt that by the time you got out to 30-40-50 years the quality line for the well stored wine would be higher than the poorly stored wine. But are there points earlier, say between 10 and 20 years old, where the poorly stored wine would be ‘better’/more interesting due to accelerated aging?

I have my own experience with this, as around 2010 I fell in love with the 1995 Mondavi Reserve, a wine that is available at a very reasonable price from many different sources. I have probably had this same bottle from like a half a dozen different auction sources now, from Winebid to higher end sources with clear provenance. The bottles differ incredibly by source. I’ve had some bottles that taste maybe five years old, with the palate dominated by smooth rich fruit, oak overtones not yet absorbed, and pipe tobacco and chocolate elements. I’ve had other bottles that tasted borderline over the hill with their best days clearly behind them (which should not be the case for 95 MR).

But the very best bottles I had had have come from what I’m pretty sure was the worst stored source (yes, Winebid) – they were in a beautifully nuanced stage with the fruit very much present but delicate and gentle, the flavors complex and seamless, and this beautiful fresh spring water type finish. The bottles that tasted very young were excellent but just not as complex, ‘melded’ or seamless as some of the more ‘advanced’ bottles, so not as good for my taste. I don’t know if more years of ‘perfect’ storage will bring them to the same aging point I enjoyed in the other bottles, or whether the aging process will fundamentally differ.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Another data point from the same NYC closet:

1983 Maximim Grunhauser (von Schubert) “Auslese 98” fuder, served blindly: Rose/pink color; Lightly sweet, intense/concentrated, but light on its feet. Perfect balance of thinning sweetness and acidity. Just a joy. 95 points and WOTN for me.

Stored in that apartment closet since release. Would this have been better with good storage? Probably. But it was still a treasure. I’m sure the sugar helped preserve it.

Go figure.

OK John, I’m glad to see your friends finally out of the closet.

(Of course, storage isn’t the only variable. Two or three years ago, I and another member each served the same Willi Shaeffer Graacher Kabinett back to back. Each bottle was purchased at Crush a day or two before the tasting. No one thought they were the same wine when served. They were very different. No storage issue there. Go figure!)

This happens a lot if you serve the same wine but people don’t know it. I was drinking a Vermentino the other day and thought it was pretty nice.

The restaurant owner came over and said I needed to try a particular wine so he brought me a glass. I told him it was pretty good and seemed similar but a bit brighter than the one I’d just had. He asked what I had. I told him and he said “Oh. That’s the same wine.”

Same glass, same bottle. I could have come up with the usual excuses - time the wine was open, warmer, palate fatigue, whatever else. But it was just my poor tasting ability and the power of suggestion. I thought I was supposed to find something different so I did.

I’m with you about storage though - I’d just as soon have all my wines in a nice cool place, but if I can’t and they don’t sit in 90 degrees for days at a time, I guess I’m OK.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
GregT:

I’m with you about storage though - I’d just as soon have all my wines in a nice cool place, but if I can’t and they don’t sit in 90 degrees for days at a time, I guess I’m OK.

I take comfort in this thought since I was away when it heated up in NYC last week and I had a case of good stuff sitting out in the apartment.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
Fun read, shows anything is possible.
Entertaining anecdotal report but N=1 and no control group is unlikely to pass peer review.

About a dozen or so years ago, I went to a restaurant in Vermont, and got a 1998 Dead Arm Shiraz that was excellent. I took my second trip to that restaurant about a year ago. I got their last bottle of that very same wine (and I think likely from that very same batch). They store their wines in the restaurant at room temperature. The wine was drinkable but just barely. Clearly way over the hill. About six months prior I had a bottle that was stored properly and it was drinking perfectly. In my N=1 that showed me the important of good storage conditions. (of course, Aussie Shiraz is probably not built to handle abuse the was Bordeaux was back in the 60’s either)

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
David Glasser:

Fun read, shows anything is possible.
Entertaining anecdotal report but N=1 and no control group is unlikely to pass peer review.

Well, it’s N=2 from that closet in the last month. As I said, I’m sure these would have been better with proper storage and I’m not advocating this kind of storage. The point is that they were not impossibly past-it, even though the storage was about as bad as you could imagine and we’re talking about 50+ years of storage in the first case and ~30 years in the second case. That’s why the heading – we can be a little less neurotic.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I have tasted good and bad wines from poor storage. For my own wines, I will continue not to have wines shipped to me in the summer and will continue to store them under temperature controlled conditions.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
For what it’s worth, I’ve had several bottles of the 1961 Beausejour, every time hoping for a great one, and experimenting with a variety of decanting regimes. All came from a cool UK cellar. And all tasted pretty much like the wine you describe. In fact, you were either more tolerant than me, or got luckier, because mine have tended to end up in the kitchen. An older friend tells me they did better in '64 for what that’s worth.

I’m assuming this was the property that’s now Beaujour Bécot. The 1961 Beasejour Duffau Lagarose is terrific and will cruise along for another few decades!

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked: I have had a number of bottles from the 90s and younger with good appearance that are heat damaged, ie kept at room temperature. The definition of “drinkable/enjoyable” varies a lot depend on the individual.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

In fact, you were either more tolerant than me, or got luckier, because mine have tended to end up in the kitchen…

I’m assuming this was the property that’s now Beaujour Bécot.

I’m pretty _in_tolerant of very tired wines – coffee notes, oxidation. This bottle wasn’t that far along that path, remarkably.

It was the Becot, as I said above.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

[June 3, 2016] Another data point from the same NYC closet:

1983 Maximim Grunhauser (von Schubert) “Auslese 98” fuder, served blindly: Rose/pink color; Lightly sweet, intense/concentrated, but light on its feet. Perfect balance of thinning sweetness and acidity. Just a joy. 95 points and WOTN for me.

Stored in that apartment closet since release. Would this have been better with good storage? Probably. But it was still a treasure. I’m sure the sugar helped preserve it.

Go figure.

Same year, different bottling, same closet last night:
1983 Maximim Grunhauser (von Schubert) “Auslese 190” fuder, served blindly: Lovely, delicate nose with tangerine scents. In the mouth, light, slightly sweet, delicate. Clearly quite old. Tangerine flavors. Fading gracefully, but without a very distinctive profile. (We had a hard time guessing where this was from.) Long, rich, concentrated finish. 90-ish points for me.

Once again, I’m sure this would have been better with proper storage, but a darned good showing for 43+ years in a closet.

I don’t know if the 98 fuder is supposed to be better than the 190.

posts.name: todd waldmann
posts.cooked:
A friend of mine was gifted his father’s cellar- lots of 60s Bordeaux and some Burgundy. Once his father lost interest in wine, the wine was stored in his garage, in San Jose, and regularly exposed to temperatures in the 80s and higher. It remained there for 15+ years. I had the chance to drink a bunch of those wines early in my wine education (in the mid 90s). What we found that there were some sublime bottles, including '61 and '62 Lafite. There were some bottles that were utterly undrinkable. Most were somewhere in between. Even 2 or 3 bottles (out of a case) of '66 Pierre Bouree Gevrey-Chambertin were spectacular examples of perfectly mature Burgundy.

While the number of wines that weren’t great outnumbered the really good ones, a surprising number of bottles were very good or great. Wine is more resilient than we seem to think and it is very easy to become overly neurotic when it comes to wine storage conditions.

posts.name: Victor Hong
posts.cooked:
todd waldmann:

… it is very easy to become overly neurotic when it comes to wine storage conditions.
